extra.message:
SQL Injection in ID Parameter

The 'id' parameter is directly concatenated into a SQL query without parameterization or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract, modify, or delete database contents. The query 'SELECT ... WHERE id=' + params[id] allows for UNION-based data extraction, authentication bypass, and database manipulation.

Impact: Complete database compromise, authentication bypass, data theft

Fix: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE id=?", (params['id'],))

SQL Injection in ID Parameter

The 'id' parameter is directly concatenated into a SQL query without parameterization or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract, modify, or delete database contents. The query 'SELECT ... WHERE id=' + params[id] allows for UNION-based data extraction, authentication bypass, and database manipulation.

Impact: Complete database compromise, authentication bypass, data theft

Fix: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE id=?", (params['id'],))

SQL Injection in ID Parameter

The 'id' parameter is directly concatenated into a SQL query without parameterization or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract, modify, or delete database contents. The query 'SELECT ... WHERE id=' + params[id] allows for UNION-based data extraction, authentication bypass, and database manipulation.

Impact: Complete database compromise, authentication bypass, data theft

Fix: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE id=?", (params['id'],))

Insecure Deserialization via Pickle

User-supplied base64-like data is passed to pickle.loads() without validation. Python pickle deserialization can execute arbitrary code through crafted payloads (via __reduce__ methods). The RCE payload shown in CASES demonstrates this with 'cos system...'.

Impact: Remote code execution, full system compromise

Fix: Never deserialize user-controlled data with pickle. Use JSON for data serialization. If pickle is absolutely necessary, implement cryptographic signing (HMAC) to verify data integrity

OS Command Injection in Domain Lookup

User-controlled 'domain' parameter is concatenated into a shell command and executed with shell=True. This allows arbitrary command execution (e.g., '; cat /etc/passwd' or '& calc.exe'). The subprocess.run call with shell=True interprets the entire string as a shell command.

Impact: Full remote code execution, server compromise

Fix: Use shell=False and pass arguments as a list: subprocess.run(['nslookup', params['domain']], shell=False, ...). Validate input against a strict whitelist (e.g., valid domain regex)

Arbitrary Code Execution via Exec

The 'include' parameter fetches file contents (local or remote via URL) and passes them to exec() with a constructed environment. This allows attackers to execute arbitrary Python code by including malicious files (Remote File Inclusion -> Code Execution).

Impact: Full remote code execution, complete server compromise

Fix: Remove exec() entirely. If dynamic code execution is required, use restricted sandboxes (e.g., RestrictedPython) or safer interpreters. Never execute code from user-controlled sources

DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting

JavaScript in HTML_PREFIX extracts 'lang' from URL hash, URL decodes it, and writes it to DOM via document.write() without sanitization. Payload: #lang=<script>alert(1)</script>

Impact: Session hijacking, client-side code execution, credential theft

Fix: Use textContent instead of innerHTML/document.write. Sanitize input using DOMPurify or similar client-side library before DOM insertion.

DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting

JavaScript in HTML_PREFIX extracts 'lang' from URL hash, URL decodes it, and writes it to DOM via document.write() without sanitization. Payload: #lang=<script>alert(1)</script>

Impact: Session hijacking, client-side code execution, credential theft

Fix: Use textContent instead of innerHTML/document.write. Sanitize input using DOMPurify or similar client-side library before DOM insertion.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

When the 'path' parameter contains '://', urllib.request.urlopen() is used to fetch the resource. This allows attackers to force the server to make requests to internal services (localhost, cloud metadata endpoints like 169.254.169.254) or external malicious sites.

Impact: Access to internal services, cloud metadata theft, port scanning

Fix: Implement strict URL validation with allowlists (scheme, host). Block private IP ranges and localhost. Use a dedicated HTTP client with restricted network access

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

When the 'path' parameter contains '://', urllib.request.urlopen() is used to fetch the resource. This allows attackers to force the server to make requests to internal services (localhost, cloud metadata endpoints like 169.254.169.254) or external malicious sites.

Impact: Access to internal services, cloud metadata theft, port scanning

Fix: Implement strict URL validation with allowlists (scheme, host). Block private IP ranges and localhost. Use a dedicated HTTP client with restricted network access

XML External Entity Injection (XXE)

The XML parser is explicitly configured to load DTDs and resolve external entities (load_dtd=True, resolve_entities=True, no_network=False). This allows attackers to read local files (file:///etc/passwd) or make HTTP requests to internal services via entity references.

Impact: Arbitrary file read, SSRF, DoS (billion laughs), information disclosure

Fix: Disable DTD loading and entity resolution: lxml.etree.XMLParser(load_dtd=False, resolve_entities=False, no_network=True)

XPath Injection

User-controlled 'name' parameter is directly interpolated into an XPath query without sanitization. Attackers can inject XPath syntax to bypass authentication, extract unauthorized data, or perform boolean/time-based blind attacks (as hinted in CASES).

Impact: Authentication bypass, unauthorized data access via XML

Fix: Use parameterized XPath queries if available, or implement strict input validation/whitelisting. Escape single quotes and backslashes in user input

Remote File Inclusion (RFI)

The include parameter accepts HTTP/HTTPS URLs which are fetched and executed, allowing attackers to host malicious code externally and execute it on the server.

Impact: Remote code execution, server compromise.

Fix: Remove URL support from file inclusion. Implement strict allowlist of allowed local files only, or better, remove the feature entirely.

Insecure Session Generation

Session IDs generated using random.sample() which uses Mersenne Twister (not cryptographically secure). Also missing HttpOnly and Secure flags.

Impact: Session prediction and hijacking

Fix: Use secrets.token_urlsafe(32) and add HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict flags

HTTP Response Splitting / Header Injection

The 'charset' parameter from user input is directly concatenated into the Content-Type HTTP header without validation. If an attacker injects CRLF sequences (\r\n), they can inject arbitrary headers or split the response (HTTP Response Splitting).

Impact: Cache poisoning, XSS, session fixation via injected headers

Fix: Validate charset against a whitelist (e.g., 'utf-8', 'iso-8859-1'). Reject any input containing newline characters (\r, \n) or use strict pattern matching

HTTP Parameter Pollution

The parameter parsing logic joins multiple parameters with the same name using commas. Combined with the SQL injection in the login form, this allows authentication bypass by splitting SQL injection payloads across multiple password parameters.

Fix: Reject requests with duplicate parameter names or process only the first occurrence consistently.

Denial of Service via Memory Exhaustion

The 'size' parameter creates a string by repeating '#' characters size*size times without bounds checking. A large size value (e.g., 9999999) causes massive memory allocation and potential crash.

Impact: Application crash, server unavailability, resource exhaustion

Fix: Implement strict limits on the size parameter (e.g., max 100). Use streaming/chunked responses for large data generation instead of creating massive strings in memory

Missing CSRF Protection

Comment submission and other state-changing operations lack CSRF tokens, allowing attackers to perform actions on behalf of authenticated users.

Impact: Unauthorized actions performed via cross-site request forgery.

Fix: Implement CSRF tokens for all state-changing operations and validate Origin/Referer headers.

Missing CSRF Protection

Comment submission and other state-changing operations lack CSRF tokens, allowing attackers to perform actions on behalf of authenticated users.

Impact: Unauthorized actions performed via cross-site request forgery.

Fix: Implement CSRF tokens for all state-changing operations and validate Origin/Referer headers.

Open Redirect

The 'redir' parameter is inserted into a meta refresh tag without validation. Attackers can redirect users to malicious phishing sites (e.g., redir=https://evil.com).

Impact: Phishing attacks, credential theft via malicious redirects

Fix: Validate redirect URLs against a whitelist of allowed destinations or use relative paths only. Implement a confirmation page for external redirects

JSONP Callback Injection

The callback parameter is reflected directly without validation, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript via JSONP responses (reflected XSS).

Impact: Cross-site scripting, data exfiltration via JSONP hijacking.

Fix: Validate callback against strict allowlist of valid function names (alphanumeric only) or disable JSONP entirely.

Information Disclosure via Verbose Error Messages

Full stack traces and exception details are exposed to users when errors occur, revealing internal application structure, file paths, and implementation details that aid attackers.

Fix: Implement generic error pages for users. Log detailed errors internally but display only generic messages like 'An error occurred' to users.
